name: European Union
headline: The big rulebook is real, but the machinery to enforce it is running late.
summary: The EU passed the world's most comprehensive AI law, but the hardest obligations were pushed to 2027–2028 and the technical standards aren't ready. The rules exist; the capacity to apply them is still being built.
morphology_plain: Open gap — ambitious on paper, lagging in practice.

name: United States (federal)
headline: Federal AI policy can flip overnight — and it did.
summary: There is no standing federal AI law; direction is set by executive orders the next administration can reverse. The Biden-era order was rescinded within hours of the 2025 transition, and the posture turned deregulatory.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: United States — California
headline: California built a binding AI law and made it stick.
summary: California's frontier-AI transparency law took effect in 2026 and survived a federal attempt to override state AI rules. Enforcement capacity is still ramping up.
morphology_plain: Open gap — ambitious on paper, lagging in practice.

name: United States — Colorado
headline: Colorado built an AI law, then walked it back before it ever applied.
summary: The original Colorado AI Act was delayed and then replaced with a narrower version before it could take effect — capacity that was being built, then scaled back.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: China
headline: Cleared before launch — and what slips through stays out of sight.
summary: China controls AI upfront: services must be filed and approved before going live, so rules and practice line up at the gate. Where use breaks the rules it is hidden rather than open — official compliance on the surface, quiet workarounds underneath.
morphology_plain: Controlled upfront — systems cleared before launch.

name: United Kingdom
headline: Deliberately light — oversight is left to existing regulators.
summary: The UK has chosen not to pass a broad AI law, distributing oversight across existing regulators instead. This is a settled choice, not a transition.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: India
headline: A deliberate decision not to regulate first.
summary: India issued voluntary guidelines and has explicitly chosen not to lead with binding regulation. Light-touch by design.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Singapore
headline: Light by design, and good at it.
summary: Singapore governs AI through well-developed voluntary frameworks rather than a binding law, a model it has run since 2019. Stable, not a way-station.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Japan
headline: A law with no penalties — cooperation over coercion.
summary: Japan's AI Promotion Act is in force but carries no monetary penalties, relying on reputation and cooperation. Innovation-first by design.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Australia
headline: Considered mandatory guardrails, then chose not to.
summary: Australia weighed binding AI rules and, in its Dec 2025 National AI Plan, decided against them — moving from 'a law may be coming' to a deliberate light-touch stance.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Mexico
headline: A built oversight body was dismantled.
summary: Mexico dissolved its independent data-protection authority in 2024–2025, dismantling capacity that already existed — a regression, not mere absence.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Brazil
headline: A comprehensive bill is one vote away.
summary: Brazil's Senate approved a broad AI bill; a decisive lower-house vote was scheduled for May 2026. If enacted, enforcement will likely ramp gradually.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Chile
headline: A bill is advancing through the second chamber.
summary: Chile's AI bill, modeled on the EU and UNESCO, cleared the lower house in late 2025 and sits in the Senate. Not yet law.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Colombia
headline: Many proposals, no consolidated law.
summary: Colombia has 20+ competing AI proposals and no consolidated statute — a fragmented pipeline that may not converge soon.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Indonesia
headline: Build fast, govern light.
summary: Indonesia governs AI through a ministerial circular while a stronger framework is pending; growth is prioritized over binding rules.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Nigeria
headline: Just getting started, with a strategy in place.
summary: Nigeria finalized a national AI strategy in 2025 and has data-protection law operative, but binding AI rules are still nascent.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Kenya
headline: Early days, guided by a national strategy.
summary: Kenya launched a 2025–2030 AI strategy, developed alongside UNESCO's readiness tooling; capacity is still forming.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: South Africa
headline: A draft framework, not yet binding.
summary: South Africa has a draft national AI policy framework and relies on its data-protection law as a backstop. Early-stage.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Rwanda
headline: A national policy in early implementation.
summary: Rwanda adopted a national AI policy in 2023 and is piloting UNESCO's readiness method; capacity is nascent.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Egypt
headline: A refreshed national strategy, early stage.
summary: Egypt issued a second-edition national AI strategy in 2025; binding rules are not yet in place.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: United Arab Emirates
headline: Governance stays light while the AI build races ahead.
summary: The UAE promotes AI aggressively — even seating an AI system as a cabinet advisor — without a binding AI law. Industrial ambition far outpaces governance rules.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Saudi Arabia
headline: Principles, not binding rules.
summary: Saudi Arabia's AI principles are non-binding, with data-protection law as the backstop. Light-touch.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Israel
headline: Light-touch in civilian life, closed in defense.
summary: Israel's civilian AI policy is deliberately light, while its defense use is closed to outside view by construction.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Vietnam
headline: Looked light — then passed a full, binding law.
summary: Vietnam appeared developmental and light until it enacted a comprehensive, risk-tiered AI law in late 2025 (in force March 2026). The clearest case that 'light now' doesn't mean 'light forever'.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Switzerland
headline: A sectoral, light-touch choice.
summary: Switzerland decided in 2025 to handle AI through existing sectoral law rather than a new horizontal statute. Deliberate.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: South Korea
headline: A comprehensive law just came into force.
summary: South Korea's AI Basic Act took effect in January 2026, with a compute threshold set by decree. The law is in force; the enforcement apparatus is still young.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Turkey
headline: A bill that's been pending for years.
summary: Turkey's AI bill has sat unpassed since 2024 — 'a law is coming' that keeps not arriving.
morphology_plain: Light by design, or not built yet — little binding rule in place.

name: Russia
headline: Closed to outside view.
summary: Russia's real AI governance cannot be assessed from outside; the opacity is deliberate. Excluded from scored comparison.
morphology_plain: Closed — cannot be assessed from outside.

name: Iran
headline: Closed to outside view.
summary: Iran's real AI governance cannot be assessed from outside; the opacity is deliberate. Excluded from scored comparison.
morphology_plain: Closed — cannot be assessed from outside.

name: Anthropic
headline: Among the strongest safety practices — but still self-imposed.
summary: Anthropic has actually gated a model release on its safety standard and publishes safeguard reports, and it backed California's transparency law. Its commitments are reinforced by a governance structure but remain, ultimately, voluntary.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: OpenAI
headline: A safety framework that has been quietly loosened.
summary: OpenAI has a published preparedness framework, but some provisions were weakened without notice — a self-imposed promise it can revise.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Google DeepMind
headline: Published framework, observable gating.
summary: Google DeepMind maintains a frontier safety framework and is among the labs that published their safety policies. Self-imposed and revisable.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Microsoft
headline: Framework in place, partly self-reported.
summary: Microsoft publishes a safety framework and took on additional summit commitments; verification is largely self-reported.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Meta
headline: Open-weights promise, since walked back.
summary: Meta's defining open-weights stance was quietly abandoned with its first closed frontier model, and frontier models shipped without comparable safety artifacts.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: xAI
headline: Promised a safety policy, then missed it — twice.
summary: xAI's frontier safety policy was a draft it never finalized, and a flagship model shipped without a system card. The promise effectively never bound anything.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Amazon
headline: Published a policy; thin track record.
summary: Amazon is among the labs that published a safety framework, but evidence of real gating is limited.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: NVIDIA
headline: Published a policy; thin track record.
summary: NVIDIA published a safety framework; evidence of realization is limited.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Cohere
headline: Enterprise-focused, with a real framework.
summary: Cohere published an enterprise safety framework in 2025; its governance is realized partly through enterprise contracts.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: AI21 Labs
headline: Transparent, but not a frontier-safety framework.
summary: AI21 is transparent, but transparency is not the same as a frontier-safety structure with thresholds — so its safety posture is minimal.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Zhipu
headline: The one Chinese summit signatory — realized through China's gate.
summary: Zhipu signed the Seoul commitments and realizes governance via China's registration regime.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Moonshot
headline: Keeps its safety posture undisclosed.
summary: Moonshot maintains a non-disclosure posture on safety governance.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Baidu
headline: Governed through China's registration gate.
summary: Baidu's governance is realized through China's pre-deployment filing regime.
morphology_plain: Controlled upfront — systems cleared before launch.

name: 01.AI
headline: Signed commitments, thin realization.
summary: 01.AI signed international commitments; evidence of realization is limited.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: G42
headline: A national champion fused with the UAE state.
summary: G42 operates as a double act with the UAE state and is among the labs that published a safety policy.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Naver
headline: State-aligned, thin realization.
summary: Naver is state-aligned and among the published-policy labs; realization evidence is limited.
morphology_plain: Self-imposed and reversible — a private promise that can be dropped.

name: Tencent
headline: Safety internals not visible from outside.
summary: Tencent's safety governance cannot be assessed externally.
morphology_plain: Closed — cannot be assessed from outside.

name: HUMAIN
headline: A Saudi sovereign vehicle, closed to view.
summary: HUMAIN is a Saudi sovereign AI vehicle whose internals are closed by construction.
morphology_plain: Closed — cannot be assessed from outside.

name: EU high-risk enterprise deployer
headline: EU high-risk users face binding duties — and aren't ready.
summary: Enterprises deploying high-risk AI in the EU face binding obligations from August 2026, but their own readiness lags. The rules are arriving faster than the practice.
morphology_plain: Open gap — ambitious on paper, lagging in practice.

name: US HR / employment deployer
headline: Using AI in hiring can create real legal exposure.
summary: US employers using AI hiring tools face liability — a court let an age-discrimination case proceed against a vendor on an agent theory, exposing thousands of employer-deployers.
morphology_plain: Inversion — stronger in practice than on paper, via courts.

name: Public-administration decision deployer
headline: Public-sector AI failures stayed hidden until they blew up.
summary: Government automated-decision systems (Dutch SyRI and benefits scandals, Australia's Robodebt) ran compliant on paper until harm surfaced — a hidden gap.
morphology_plain: Hidden gap — compliant on the surface, divergent underneath.

name: Financial-services model-risk deployer
headline: The most mature AI governance is in banks.
summary: Financial-services firms govern AI under decades-old model-risk rules — the highest-realization case in the Atlas. The idea that 'deployers always lag' is false here.
morphology_plain: Aligned — rules and practice already match.

name: Consumer-facing AI deployer
headline: Companies are liable for what their AI tells customers.
summary: Consumer-facing deployers carry output liability — an airline was held responsible for its chatbot's wrong answer.
morphology_plain: Inversion — stronger in practice than on paper, via courts.

name: Chinese registered-services deployer
headline: Chinese deployers inherit control from the filing regime.
summary: Deployment in China is gated by which models are officially filed, so deployers inherit upfront control.
morphology_plain: Controlled upfront — systems cleared before launch.

name: Rome Call (interfaith)
headline: A faith-led ethical call that companies signed too.
summary: The Rome Call is an interfaith AI-ethics commitment joined by major tech firms — influence through cultural and corporate endorsement rather than law.

name: Islamic-civilizational
headline: Islamic-civilizational grounding for AI ethics.
summary: Rooted in Islamic tradition, this vision grounds AI ethics for Gulf states through religious and sovereign endorsement.

name: UNESCO
headline: The closest thing to a global AI-ethics agreement.
summary: UNESCO's 2021 Recommendation is the one near-universal AI-ethics instrument, adopted by ~193 states and diffused through national strategies and readiness assessments.

name: OECD / G7
headline: The democracies' shared principles.
summary: The OECD/G7 principles set a common baseline for democracies, with voluntary reporting layered on top.

name: Council of Europe
headline: The vision that becomes binding law.
summary: The Council of Europe's Framework Convention turns AI-rights principles into a binding treaty — the EU ratified it in May 2026.

name: IEEE
headline: Ethics from the engineering profession.
summary: IEEE grounds AI ethics in professional, technical standards — legitimacy from the profession, not states.

name: The three gravities
headline: Three centers of gravity pulling the field.
summary: Brussels (rights), Washington (markets and security), and Beijing (developmental sovereignty) are the three forces whose pull explains most national choices.

name: AI-safety / frontier community
headline: The frontier-safety community.
summary: Researchers and labs committed to catastrophic-risk foresight, expressed through summit commitments and lab self-binding — a thin institutional base but real influence.

name: Global South / development-justice
headline: For the Global South, exclusion is the real risk.
summary: A development-justice vision holding that the danger that matters is being left out, not extinction — voiced through coalitions and the development agenda.
